feedback.text: The Union of the Slovak Republic welcomes the European Commission’s proposal for a Digital Justice Act, which responds to proven shortcomings in consumer protection in the online environment. We fully support the objectives of the initiative, in particular: prohibition of manipulative design features (dark patterns), liability for unmarked or harmful influencer marketing, protection against addictive design of applications and digital services, stricter regulation of personalisation of prices and advertising, protection of minors as a horizontal priority in all measures. 1. Lack of awareness among Slovak consumers In Slovakia, the topic of manipulative digital practices in the media appears minimally. There is a lack of investigative articles, systematic analyses and public campaigns to alert ordinary people to risks such as dark patterns, personalised manipulation or unlabelled advertising. This communication deficit results in: low awareness of the risks of digital behaviour, weaker ability to defend and exercise their rights, less social support for enforcing the new rules. In the final proposal, we recommend that the Commission: it supported Member States in educational and information activities, created a single European information portal with examples of unfair digital practices, strengthened the role of civil society in raising awareness. 2. The monitoring of influencer marketing confirms the extent of the problem according to the IAB Slovakia monitoring (Q3 2024) up to 29 % of the commercial contributions of Slovak influencers were not correctly labelled as advertising. Such a practice leads to a systematic misleading of consumers. We therefore support the European Commission’s proposal to: mandatory use of tools such as paid partnerships, sanctions for unmarked advertising, liability of platforms hosting the advertisement. 3. The need to accurately name manipulative techniques The experience of BEUC member organisations (e.g. Shein, Tem) confirms the need for a clear legal definition of the selected manipulative techniques, including: ‘countdown timers’, hiding the possibility of cancelling orders, so-called ‘confirming shaming of guilt manipulation in the rejection of the tender’. These practices should be specifically mentioned and prohibited in legislation. We consider the Commission’s proposal to be a particularly necessary step towards strengthening consumer rights in the digital environment. At the same time, we stress that without: broad and systematic outreach, more active national supervisory authorities, and strengthening the powers of consumer organisations will not make a real difference on the ground.
